content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

How Pinpoint smuggles DOM context out of VS Code’s Integrated Browser using a debugger, the clipboard, and a couple of tricks no API was designed for.
If you build frontends with an AI agent, whatever lives in your sidebar, you know this conversation:
> Me: The header looks broken on mobile.
> Agent: greps for “header”, finds four components named Header, picks the wrong one, confidently adjusts padding that was never the problem.
The agent can’t see the rendered page. It sees source files. Everything I know from a glance at DevTools, the actual element, the matched CSS rules, the resolved values, which variable is feeding that wrong color, the agent has to reconstruct by reading code and guessing.
My workaround used to be manual: inspect the element in DevTools, copy the outer HTML, copy the relevant styles, paste a wall of text into the chat. It works, but it floods the prompt, and by the third element you stop bothering.
So I built Pinpoint: click an element in VS Code’s Integrated Browser, and its full context , selector, URL, outer HTML, dimensions, matched and inherited CSS, resolved values, CSS variables, is written to a neatly named file, with an `@`-mention of it sitting on your clipboard. Click, paste, done.
Simple idea. Almost nothing about building it was simple, because almost nothing I needed had an API. This article is about the workarounds.
The Integrated Browser has no DOM API
VS Code recently shipped an experimental Integrated Browser, a real browser tab inside the editor, which is wonderful for agent-driven frontend work: the agent edits, the page hot-reloads, you never leave the editor.
But it’s experimental, and the extension API surface for it is essentially: nothing. No way to read the page, inject a script, or listen to clicks.
There is one back door: the browser tab is debuggable. VS Code’s built-in js-debug can attach to it like it attaches to Chrome. And a debug session will happily evaluate arbitrary JavaScript in the page:
const started = await vscode.debug.startDebugging(undefined, {
type: 'editor-browser',
request: 'attach',
name: 'Pinpoint (Integrated Browser)',
urlFilter: '*',
timeout: 4000
});// later, on the session:
await session.customRequest('evaluate', {
expression: pickerSource, // the entire picker script, stringified
context: 'repl'
});
So Pinpoint’s “arm the picker” flow is: attach the debugger, evaluate a ~400-line picker script into the page, and then , this part matters, disconnect immediately. The picker lives entirely in the page once injected; keeping the debug session alive would leave VS Code’s floating debug toolbar hovering over your editor like a confused ghost. Attach, inject, detach, all within a second or two.
(There’s a pile of fiddly detail here I’ll spare you: suppressing the debug toolbar and status bar, racing `stopDebugging` against a timeout because it never settles when the tab is blank, retrying the evaluate until the page confirms `PINPOINT_ACTIVE`. Experimental features keep you humble.)
The picker: an overlay the page can’t touch
The injected script is a classic element picker: crosshair cursor, a highlight box that follows the pointer, a label showing the selector and dimensions. Two implementation choices earn their keep:
Closed shadow DOM. The overlay lives in a `div` with `all: initial`, maximum z-index, `pointer-events: none`, and a *closed* shadow root. The page’s own styles can’t leak in, and the page’s scripts can’t poke at it.
Capture-phase listeners. Clicks are intercepted at the window with `addEventListener(‘click’, onClick, true)` plus `stopImmediatePropagation()`, so picking an element never actually triggers the page’s own click handlers. You can pick a delete button without pressing it.
Capturing CSS the way DevTools shows it
The HTML part of the capture is trivial (`outerHTML`, truncated at 60 KB). The CSS part is where the value is, and it took the most iteration. Pinpoint captures four layers:
Matched rules. Walk `document.styleSheets`, recurse into media queries (only ones that currently match), and test `element.matches(rule.selectorText)`. Cross-origin stylesheets throw — wrap in try/catch and move on. I keep the *authored* declarations (`rule.style.cssText`) rather than iterating properties, because iteration expands shorthands and, fatally for modern codebases, resolves away `var()` references. If the rule says `color: var( — brand)`, the agent should see that.
Inherited rules. Walk up the ancestor chain and collect matched rules, filtered to properties that actually inherit (`color`, `font-*`, `line-height`, …). This is how DevTools’ “Inherited from div.card” section works.
Resolved values. `getComputedStyle` returns 350+ properties, nearly all noise. The trick: build a hidden iframe, create a *pristine* element of the same tag inside it, and diff the two computed styles. Only properties that something actually set survive. Then fold longhand groups back into shorthands (`margin-top/right/bottom/left` → `margin`) so the output reads like CSS a human wrote.
CSS variables. Regex the captured rule text for `var( — …)` references, then resolve each one via `getComputedStyle`. When the agent sees `background: var( — surface-2)`, the report also tells it ` — surface-2: #f6f8fa`.
The result reads like the Styles panel, in Markdown, in the agent’s context window.
Getting the data out: the clipboard is an IPC channel now
Here’s the problem I created for myself: by the time you *click* an element, the debugger is long gone. The picker is orphaned inside the page with no channel back to the extension. The Integrated Browser’s page can’t reach the extension host — no `postMessage` bridge, nothing.
Except… both sides can touch the clipboard.
So the picker serializes the payload and copies it with a marker prefix:
await navigator.clipboard.writeText(
`PINPOINT_CONTEXT:${JSON.stringify(payload)}`
);And the extension, while the picker is armed, polls the clipboard every 300 ms:
const text = await vscode.env.clipboard.readText();
if (text.startsWith('PINPOINT_CONTEXT:')) {
const payload = JSON.parse(text.slice(MARKER.length));
const {mention} = writeContextFile(payload);
await vscode.env.clipboard.writeText(`@${mention}`);
}Is polling the clipboard as an IPC channel ugly? Absolutely. Is it robust, permissionless, and does it degrade gracefully (worst case, the user just… has the payload on their clipboard)? Also yes. The watcher swaps the raw JSON for something human-usable within a third of a second, so you never actually see the marker blob.
Why a file, and why it must live in the workspace
Early versions pasted the whole report into the chat. Terrible: a single element’s context can be a few thousand tokens of CSS, and it buries whatever you were actually asking.
The fix is to write the report to a Markdown file and reference it — coding agents understand `@path/to/file` mentions and attach the file as context instead of flooding the message.
But there’s a trap: coding agents are sandboxed to your workspace. My first implementation wrote to the OS temp dir, and the mention silently pointed at a file the agent wasn’t allowed to read. So the reports live in a `.pinpoint/` folder *inside the workspace* — with its own `.gitignore` so it never pollutes the repo, and a 24-hour pruning policy so it never accumulates.
The filenames matter more than you’d think, because a human reads them in the chat input. An early version produced `element-span.weather__summary.weather__summary__bottom-1784388238159.md` — technically unique, practically unreadable. Now Pinpoint takes the first id or class off the selector and turns it into a name: `weather-summary.md`, and `weather-summary-2.md` if you capture it again. One scar with a story survives in the naming rules: no `#` characters, because agents parse `#` inside an @-mention as a line-range separator (`@file.md#L10–20`) and truncate the path.
The feature I deleted: auto-attach
For a while, Pinpoint did more. It detected open agent chats and pushed the capture straight into them — a command here, a sleight of hand with a preview editor there, a gear menu to choose which agent got the file. Zero-paste. The demo was great.
The maintenance story wasn’t. Every agent needed its own undocumented integration, discovered by reading other extensions’ manifests. Chats docked in a sidebar were invisible to the tab API, so half the code existed to guess at what it couldn’t see. And each integration was one extension update away from silently breaking.
Meanwhile the fallback path — mention on the clipboard, you press paste — worked everywhere, for every agent, including ones I’d never heard of. So I deleted the integrations, the gear menu, and the setting that chose between them. Pinpoint now works with every AI chat precisely because it integrates with none of them. The cost is one keystroke.
What I took away from this
Experimental surfaces reward reading, not searching. Nothing here is documented. The `editor-browser` debug type came from reading VS Code’s own manifests and bundled source. If you’re building on top of undocumented surfaces, the manifest is your API reference.
Design every step to fail into something useful. Debugger attach fails? Clear error, no zombie toolbar. Clipboard write fails? Fall back to `execCommand(‘copy’)`, and past that, at least copy the bare selector. Users mostly never see any of this — which is the point.
The ugly channel that exists beats the clean channel that doesn’t. The clipboard-as-IPC design offended me for about a day. Then it survived every edge case I threw at it while the “proper” alternatives remained imaginary. Ship the marker string.
A paste you can trust beats an integration you can’t. The flashiest feature was the first one worth deleting. If the universal path is one keystroke worse than the bespoke one, the universal path wins.
Pinpoint is open source and on the VS Code Marketplace. Click an element, paste the mention, ask your agent to fix it , and enjoy the small luxury of never typing “no, the *other* header” again.
